name: Anglo-Australian Telescope
description: A 3.9m equatorially mounted telescope at Siding Spring Observatory. Pioneered multi-fiber spectroscopy and produced landmark galaxy surveys.

name: Abastumani Astrophysical Observatory
description: Georgian national observatory in the Lesser Caucasus mountains, founded in 1932. Known for atmospheric physics and stellar polarimetry research.

name: Atacama Cosmology Telescope
description: A 6m telescope in the Atacama Desert designed to measure the cosmic microwave background at fine angular scales. Key data for cosmological parameters.

name: Algonquin Radio Observatory
description: Historic Canadian radio observatory. The 46m dish participated in some of the first VLBI experiments and continues as a research and educational facility.

name: Atacama Large Millimeter/submillimeter Array
description: The world's most powerful millimeter/submillimeter telescope array on the Chajnantor Plateau. 66 antennas achieve extraordinary resolution for studying cold cosmic objects.

name: ANTARES Neutrino Telescope
description: An undersea neutrino telescope in the Mediterranean off Toulon. Operated from 2008-2022 and pioneered deep-sea neutrino detection, succeeded by KM3NeT.

name: Apache Point Observatory
description: Home of the Sloan Digital Sky Survey telescope that created the most detailed three-dimensional maps of the universe, cataloging millions of galaxies and quasars.

name: Arcetri Astrophysical Observatory
description: Historic INAF research institute in Florence, founded in 1872 near Galileo's last home. Leading center for adaptive optics development and infrared astronomy.

name: Arecibo Observatory
description: The iconic 305m radio telescope collapsed in December 2020 after cable failures. Its discoveries included the first binary pulsar and first exoplanets around a pulsar.

name: Asiago Astrophysical Observatory
description: INAF observatory in the Veneto region of Italy, operating the Copernico telescope. Active in supernova searches and transient object classification.

name: ASKAP (Australian SKA Pathfinder)
description: 36-dish radio telescope array in Western Australia's Murchison region. ASKAP's phased array feeds provide an exceptionally wide field of view.

name: Australia Telescope Compact Array
description: Six 22m antennas near Narrabri forming a radio interferometer. Part of the Australia Telescope National Facility for centimeter-wavelength observations.

name: ATLAS Mauna Loa
description: Part of the Asteroid Terrestrial-impact Last Alert System network. Scans the entire visible sky every two nights for potentially hazardous asteroids.

name: Baikal-GVD
description: A deep underwater neutrino telescope in Lake Baikal, Siberia. Uses strings of optical modules at depths up to 1366m to detect high-energy neutrinos.

name: Big Bear Solar Observatory
location: Big Bear Solar Observatory, United States
description: Located on an island in Big Bear Lake to reduce atmospheric turbulence. The Goode Solar Telescope produces the highest-resolution images of the solar surface.

name: Bohyunsan Optical Astronomy Observatory
location: Bohyunsan Optical Astronomy Observatory, South Korea
description: KASI's primary optical telescope at Bohyunsan in North Gyeongsang Province. South Korea's largest optical telescope for research observations.

name: Steward Observatory (Bok Telescope)
description: University of Arizona's Bok 2.3m telescope at Kitt Peak. Played a key role in target selection for the DESI dark energy experiment.

name: Bosque Alegre Astrophysical Station
description: The observing station of Córdoba Observatory in the Sierras de Córdoba mountains. Argentina's main optical facility for decades.

name: Bosscha Observatory
description: Indonesia's oldest modern observatory, founded in 1923 in Lembang. Operated by the Bandung Institute of Technology for research and education.

name: Byurakan Astrophysical Observatory
description: Founded by Viktor Ambartsumian. The Markarian Survey discovered UV-excess galaxies, fundamentally advancing understanding of active galactic nuclei.

name: Calar Alto Observatory
description: The largest astronomical observatory in continental Europe, located in Andalusia. The CARMENES spectrograph surveys nearby red dwarfs for exoplanets.

name: Ceduna Radio Observatory
description: University of Tasmania radio telescope in South Australia. Provides critical long baselines for Australian and southern hemisphere VLBI observations.

name: Cerro Armazones Observatory Site
description: ESO's chosen site for the Extremely Large Telescope, 20 km from Paranal. The mountain peak was leveled to create the telescope platform.

name: Cerro Murphy Observatory
description: Observatory site in the Namibian highlands with exceptional seeing and dark skies, operated by the International Amateur Sternwarte.

name: Cerro Pachón
description: Mountain-top observatory in the Chilean Andes hosting Gemini South and SOAR telescopes. Future site of the Vera C. Rubin Observatory.

name: Canada-France-Hawaii Telescope
description: A 3.6m telescope on Mauna Kea operated by Canada, France, and Hawaii. Known for wide-field imaging surveys and the MegaCam instrument.

name: Chandra X-ray Center
description: Science operations center at the Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory in Cambridge, MA. Chandra provides the sharpest X-ray images of the cosmos.

name: Observatoire de la Côte d'Azur
location: Observatoire de la Côte d'Azur, France
description: French observatory near Nice combining astronomy, geodesy, and geophysics research. Operates a satellite and lunar laser ranging station.

name: Cherenkov Telescope Array North
description: The Northern Hemisphere site of CTA at La Palma. LST-1 is already operational and has detected its first gamma-ray sources.

name: Cherenkov Telescope Array South
description: The Southern Hemisphere CTA site near Paranal, Chile. Will have over 50 telescopes spanning three sizes for comprehensive gamma-ray sky coverage.

name: Cerro Tololo Inter-American Observatory
description: NOIRLab facility in the Chilean Andes. The Blanco telescope with DECam has been pivotal for the Dark Energy Survey and gravitational wave follow-up.

name: Danish 1.54m Telescope
description: A Danish telescope at ESO La Silla used for photometry and lucky imaging. Part of the MiNDSTEp microlensing network searching for exoplanets.

name: Dominion Astrophysical Observatory
description: NRC Herzberg facility in Victoria, BC. The Plaskett telescope contributed fundamental data on stellar radial velocities and binary star systems.

name: Devasthal Observatory
description: ARIES observatory hosting Asia's largest optical telescope (3.6m DOT) and the first astronomical liquid mirror telescope (ILMT).

name: Daniel K. Inouye Solar Telescope
location: Daniel K. Inouye Solar Telescope, United States
description: The world's largest solar telescope on Haleakalā, Maui. Its 4m aperture resolves features as small as 20 km on the Sun's surface.

name: Doi Inthanon Observatory Complex
description: NARIT observatory complex at Doi Inthanon. Besides the 2.4m Thai National Telescope, hosts smaller robotic telescopes for continuous monitoring.

name: Dominion Radio Astrophysical Observatory
description: NRC facility in Penticton, BC. Home of CHIME, the Canadian Hydrogen Intensity Mapping Experiment, which has detected hundreds of fast radio bursts.

name: Effelsberg Radio Telescope
description: A 100m radio telescope near Bonn operated by the Max Planck Institute for Radio Astronomy. One of the largest fully steerable radio telescopes in the world.

name: Einstein Probe Operations
description: Chinese Academy of Sciences X-ray satellite launched in 2024, monitoring the sky for X-ray transients with novel lobster-eye optics. Discovering previously unseen X-ray phenomena.

name: Einstein Tower Solar Observatory
description: Expressionist tower telescope designed by Erich Mendelsohn in 1924 at the Leibniz Institute for Astrophysics Potsdam. An architectural and scientific landmark.

name: Extremely Large Telescope
description: ESO's next-generation telescope at Cerro Armazones, the world's largest optical/infrared telescope. First light expected around 2028.

name: European Space Astronomy Centre
description: ESA's space science operations center near Madrid. Manages XMM-Newton, Gaia, Euclid, and INTEGRAL missions, producing the most detailed star map ever.

name: European Southern Observatory HQ
description: ESO headquarters in Garching near Munich. Coordinates operations of La Silla, Paranal, ALMA, and the Extremely Large Telescope construction.

name: Swiss 1.2m Euler Telescope
description: Geneva Observatory telescope at La Silla. CORALIE spectrograph has discovered and characterized numerous exoplanets, complementing the HARPS program.

name: Five-hundred-meter Aperture Spherical Telescope
description: The world's largest single-dish radio telescope in Guizhou province. With its 500m aperture, it has discovered hundreds of new pulsars since becoming operational in 2020.

name: Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory
location: Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory, United States
description: Smithsonian Astrophysical Observatory facility on Mount Hopkins, Arizona. VERITAS array detects very-high-energy gamma rays from cosmic sources.

name: Nanshan Station (Xinjiang Astronomical Observatory)
description: CAS Xinjiang Astronomical Observatory's Nanshan station near Urumqi. Conducts both optical and radio observations with multiple instruments.

name: Gemini North
description: Part of the international Gemini Observatory on Mauna Kea. Optimized for infrared observations with a silver-coated primary mirror.

name: Gemini Observatory HQ
description: International Gemini Observatory operated by NOIRLab. The twin 8.1m telescopes provide full-sky coverage for the Gemini partnership of seven nations.

name: Gemini South
description: The southern twin of Gemini North at Cerro Pachón, Chile. Together they provide full-sky coverage for the international Gemini partnership.

name: GEO600
description: German-British gravitational wave detector near Hannover. A testbed for advanced technologies later adopted by LIGO and Virgo.

name: Giant Metrewave Radio Telescope
description: Array of 30 dishes near Pune, India. After upgrade to uGMRT, it is the world's most sensitive radio telescope at meter wavelengths.

name: Giant Magellan Telescope
description: Seven 8.4m mirrors forming a 25.4m equivalent aperture at Las Campanas. Under construction with first light expected around 2029.

name: Goldstone Deep Space Communications Complex
location: Goldstone Deep Space Communications Complex, United States
description: Part of NASA's Deep Space Network in the Mojave Desert. DSS-14 is used for both spacecraft communication and planetary radar astronomy.

name: Green Bank Observatory
description: Home of the world's largest fully steerable radio telescope (100m GBT) in the National Radio Quiet Zone of West Virginia.

name: Goddard Space Flight Center (Fermi)
location: Goddard Space Flight Center (Fermi), United States
description: NASA GSFC manages Fermi, which surveys the gamma-ray sky. Discovered enormous gamma-ray structures extending above and below the Milky Way's center.

name: Gran Telescopio Canarias
description: The world's largest single-aperture optical telescope, located at the Roque de los Muchachos Observatory in La Palma, Canary Islands.

name: Haleakalā Observatory
description: Multi-telescope facility on Maui hosting DKIST, Pan-STARRS, and ATLAS survey telescopes. Pan-STARRS discovered the first known interstellar object, 'Oumuamua.

name: Hamburg Observatory
description: University of Hamburg facility in Bergedorf. Conducted the Hamburg Quasar Survey discovering thousands of quasars and rare stellar objects.

name: Indian Astronomical Observatory
description: One of the world's highest observatory sites in Ladakh. Remote-operated 2m telescope and MACE gamma-ray telescope benefit from the dry, high-altitude conditions.

name: Hartebeesthoek Radio Astronomy Observatory
location: Hartebeesthoek Radio Astronomy Observatory, South Africa
description: South Africa's first radio astronomy observatory. The 26m telescope is a key southern VLBI station and conducts pulsar timing and maser observations.

name: Observatoire de Haute-Provence
keyDiscoveries: First exoplanet around sun-like star (51 Peg b)
description: Historic French observatory where Mayor and Queloz discovered 51 Pegasi b in 1995, the first exoplanet orbiting a sun-like star, earning the 2019 Nobel Prize.

name: HAWC Observatory
description: High-Altitude Water Cherenkov observatory on the slopes of Sierra Negra volcano. 300 large water tanks detect air showers from gamma rays and cosmic rays 24/7.

name: Haystack Observatory
description: MIT Lincoln Laboratory research facility. Major contributor to the Event Horizon Telescope and pioneer of VLBI techniques.

name: Helwan Observatory
description: Egypt's national observatory, now centered at the Kottamia Astronomical Observatory. The 1.88m telescope is the largest in the Middle East and North Africa.

name: H.E.S.S.
description: High Energy Stereoscopic System in the Namibian Khomas Highland. Five Cherenkov telescopes mapping the very-high-energy gamma-ray sky of the Southern Hemisphere.

name: Hobby-Eberly Telescope
description: A 10m segmented-mirror telescope at McDonald Observatory, Texas. Designed for spectroscopy with a fixed altitude angle and tracking by moving the instrument package.

name: IceCube Neutrino Observatory
description: A cubic-kilometer neutrino detector embedded in Antarctic ice at the South Pole. Detected the first identified source of high-energy cosmic neutrinos.

name: Indian Deep Space Network
description: ISRO's Deep Space Network at Byalalu near Bangalore. The 32m dish supports India's interplanetary missions and participates in radio astronomy.

name: Isaac Newton Telescope
description: Part of the Isaac Newton Group on La Palma. The Wide Field Camera has been used for major photometric surveys of the northern galactic plane.

name: IPAC at Caltech
description: Caltech's Infrared Processing and Analysis Center. Science center for Spitzer (retired 2020), WISE/NEOWISE, and the US Euclid data center.

name: IRAM 30m Telescope
description: The largest millimeter-wave telescope in the Northern Hemisphere, located in the Sierra Nevada. A key station for the Event Horizon Telescope.

name: Observatorio Astrofísico de Javalambre
description: CEFCA observatory in Teruel designed for large-scale photometric surveys. The J-PAS survey will map hundreds of millions of galaxies with 56 narrowband filters.

name: James Clerk Maxwell Telescope
location: James Clerk Maxwell Telescope, United Kingdom
description: The world's largest single-dish submillimeter telescope on Mauna Kea. SCUBA-2 camera revealed populations of dust-obscured galaxies in the early universe.

name: Jodrell Bank Observatory
description: UNESCO World Heritage Site home to the Lovell Telescope, one of the largest steerable radio telescopes. The e-MERLIN network provides high-resolution radio imaging.

name: KAGRA
description: The Kamioka Gravitational-Wave Detector, built underground in the Kamioka mine. The first gravitational wave detector to use cryogenic mirrors.

name: Korea Astronomy and Space Science Institute
location: Korea Astronomy and Space Science Institute, South Korea
description: South Korea's principal astronomy institute operating multiple observatories. KMTNet network (Chile, Australia, South Africa) monitors for gravitational microlensing events.

name: W. M. Keck Observatory
location: W. M. Keck Observatory, United States
description: Twin 10-meter segmented-mirror telescopes atop Mauna Kea, among the world's largest optical/infrared telescopes. Pioneered adaptive optics and interferometric techniques.

name: Kitt Peak National Observatory
location: Kitt Peak National Observatory, United States
description: NOIRLab facility on the Tohono O'odham Nation in Arizona. The Mayall telescope now hosts DESI, creating the largest 3D map of the universe.

name: KM3NeT
description: Next-generation undersea neutrino telescope in the Mediterranean. ARCA (Italy) for cosmic neutrinos and ORCA (France) for neutrino properties.

name: Konkoly Observatory
description: The principal astronomical institute of Hungary, part of the Research Centre for Astronomy and Earth Sciences. Extensive work on variable stars and asteroids.

name: La Plata Observatory
description: Historic Argentine observatory founded in 1883 in La Plata. Now primarily a research and teaching institution with contributions to stellar catalogs.

name: La Silla Observatory
description: ESO's first observatory site in Chile. Home to HARPS, the world's most successful exoplanet-hunting spectrograph, and the New Technology Telescope.

name: Swedish 1-m Solar Telescope
description: A vacuum solar telescope on La Palma achieving diffraction-limited observations through adaptive optics. Produces remarkably detailed solar images.

name: Las Campanas Observatory
description: Carnegie Institution observatory in the Chilean Andes. Home to the twin Magellan telescopes and future site of the Giant Magellan Telescope.

name: Large Binocular Telescope
description: Twin 8.4m mirrors on a single mount at Mount Graham, Arizona. When combined, equivalent to an 11.8m telescope, the largest in the world.

name: Las Cumbres Observatory (HQ)
description: A global network of robotic telescopes at 7 sites spanning both hemispheres. Provides continuous time-domain coverage for transient events and education.

name: Leoncito Astronomical Complex
description: CASLEO observatory in San Juan province near the Andes, combining excellent high-altitude conditions with dark skies for southern hemisphere observations.

name: Lick Observatory
description: UC's first permanently occupied mountain-top observatory on Mount Hamilton. The Automated Planet Finder is a robotic telescope dedicated to exoplanet searches.

name: LIGO Hanford
description: One of two LIGO detectors, in Washington state. Made the first direct detection of gravitational waves in 2015, confirming Einstein's prediction.

name: LIGO Livingston
description: The Louisiana LIGO detector. Together with Hanford, enables gravitational wave sky localization. Has detected dozens of black hole and neutron star mergers.

name: LISA Pathfinder / LISA (ESAC)
keyDiscoveries: Gravitational wave detection in space validated
description: LISA Pathfinder demonstrated the technology for space-based gravitational wave detection. LISA, launching ~2035, will detect low-frequency gravitational waves from supermassive black hole mergers.

name: Liverpool Telescope
description: The world's largest fully robotic telescope on La Palma. Specializes in rapid-response observations of gamma-ray bursts and other transient phenomena.

name: Llano de Chajnantor Observatory
description: One of the highest observatory sites on Earth in the Atacama Desert. Hosts multiple millimeter/submillimeter and CMB experiments alongside ALMA.

name: Low-Frequency Array
description: A distributed radio telescope with stations across Europe, centered in the Netherlands. Observes at the lowest radio frequencies accessible from Earth.

name: Lowell Observatory
description: Founded in 1894 by Percival Lowell. Site of Pluto's discovery in 1930 by Clyde Tombaugh. The Lowell Discovery Telescope is its modern flagship.

name: Lulin Observatory
description: National Central University observatory in Yushan National Park, the best astronomical site in East Asia. Active in comet and asteroid surveys.

name: Madrid Deep Space Communications Complex
location: Madrid Deep Space Communications Complex, Spain
description: NASA's DSN station at Robledo de Chavela near Madrid. Together with Goldstone and Canberra provides continuous deep-space communication coverage.

name: Magellan Telescopes
description: Twin 6.5m telescopes at Las Campanas Observatory. Known for excellent image quality and instruments like the Planet Finder Spectrograph.

name: MAGIC Telescopes
description: Twin 17m Cherenkov telescopes at La Palma detecting very-high-energy gamma rays. First to detect a gamma-ray burst at energies above 300 GeV.

name: Maidanak Observatory
description: One of the best astronomical sites in the CIS, in the Pamir-Alay range. Exceptional seeing conditions rivaling top sites worldwide.

name: Mauna Loa Solar Observatory
location: Mauna Loa Solar Observatory, United States
description: HAO/NCAR facility monitoring the solar corona and chromosphere. Provides continuous observations of the Sun crucial for space weather forecasting.

name: Maunakea Observatories
description: The world's premier astronomical site hosting 13 telescopes from 11 countries near the summit of Maunakea, Hawaii. Exceptional seeing and dry atmosphere.

name: McDonald Observatory
description: University of Texas observatory in the Davis Mountains. Known for excellent dark skies and public outreach programs.

name: McMath-Pierce Solar Telescope
description: Located at Kitt Peak, it was the world's largest solar telescope for decades. A distinctive design with a heliostat feeding a 60m underground optical path.

name: Medicina Radio Astronomical Station
description: INAF radio astronomy station near Bologna. The Northern Cross is one of the largest cross-type radio telescopes and a key European VLBI Network station.

name: MeerKAT
description: South African precursor to the Square Kilometre Array with 64 dishes in the Karoo. Has produced stunning radio images of the Galactic center.

name: Miyun Station
description: NAOC radio astronomy station northeast of Beijing. Operates a radio interferometric array for solar and cosmic radio observations.

name: MMT Observatory
description: A 6.5m telescope on Mount Hopkins, Arizona, jointly operated by the Smithsonian and the University of Arizona. Originally the Multiple Mirror Telescope.

name: Molonglo Observatory Synthesis Telescope
description: A large Mills Cross radio telescope near Canberra, converted to a synthesis telescope (UTMOST). Now a key facility for localizing fast radio bursts.

name: Mount John University Observatory
location: Mount John University Observatory, New Zealand
description: University of Canterbury observatory near Lake Tekapo. The MOA (Microlensing Observations in Astrophysics) project has discovered exoplanets via gravitational microlensing.

name: Mount Stromlo Observatory
description: ANU observatory near Canberra. Severely damaged in the 2003 bushfires, rebuilt as an advanced instrumentation and technology center.

name: Mount Wilson Observatory
description: Historic observatory where Edwin Hubble discovered the expanding universe. Now hosts the CHARA interferometric array achieving the highest angular resolution in optical astronomy.

name: Murchison Radio-astronomy Observatory
description: Australia's premier radio-quiet site in Western Australia. Home to MWA and ASKAP precursor arrays for the Square Kilometre Array.

name: Nanshan Radio Observatory
description: Xinjiang Astronomical Observatory facility near Urumqi. A key VLBI station in the Chinese VLBI Network and the European VLBI Network.

name: Nançay Radio Observatory
description: Paris Observatory station in Sologne. The decimetric telescope and new NenuFAR low-frequency array study pulsars, exoplanets, and the Sun.

name: Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope (GSFC)
location: Nancy Grace Roman Space Telescope (GSFC), United States
description: NASA's next flagship observatory, with Hubble-sized mirror but 100× wider field of view. Will survey dark energy, exoplanets, and infrared sky from L2 orbit.

name: National Astronomical Observatory of Japan (Mitaka)
location: National Astronomical Observatory of Japan (Mitaka), Japan
description: NAOJ headquarters in Tokyo coordinating Japanese astronomical research including Subaru, ALMA, and next-generation facilities like TMT.

name: NASA Ames Research Center (Kepler/TESS)
location: NASA Ames Research Center (Kepler/TESS), United States
description: Managed Kepler which discovered over 2,600 exoplanets. TESS, now managed jointly, is surveying the entire sky for nearby transiting exoplanets.

name: Nishi-Harima Astronomical Observatory
description: Japan's largest publicly accessible telescope in Hyogo Prefecture. The Nayuta 2m telescope is used for both research and extensive public engagement.

name: Nobeyama Radio Observatory
description: NAOJ millimeter-wave observatory at the Nobeyama highlands. The 45m telescope is the largest millimeter-wave telescope operating at sea-level-equivalent altitude.

name: NOEMA
description: IRAM's Northern Extended Millimeter Array on the Plateau de Bure in the French Alps. The most powerful millimeter interferometer in the Northern Hemisphere.

name: Northern Cross Radio Telescope
description: One of the largest cross-type radio telescopes in the world at Medicina. Currently being upgraded for fast radio burst and pulsar observations.

name: Nordic Optical Telescope
description: A 2.56m telescope on La Palma operated by Nordic countries. Known for excellent image quality and flexible scheduling for rapid follow-up observations.

name: Carter Observatory
description: New Zealand's national observatory and planetarium in Wellington, primarily focused on public engagement and science education with heritage telescopes.

name: Observatorio Cerro Mamalluca & Pangue
description: Public and scientific observatories in the Elqui Valley, taking advantage of some of the clearest skies in the world for both research and astrotourism.

name: Observatório Nacional
description: Brazil's national observatory in Rio de Janeiro, founded in 1827. Operates the Pico dos Dias Observatory with the country's largest optical telescope.

name: Pico dos Dias Observatory
description: Laboratório Nacional de Astrofísica observatory in Minas Gerais. Brazil's premier ground-based optical facility with the 1.6m Perkin-Elmer telescope.

name: Cerro Tololo/NOIRLab Chile
description: NOIRLab's flagship southern hemisphere site hosting the Blanco 4m with DECam, SOAR 4.1m, and SMARTS consortium telescopes for multi-scale observations.

name: Okayama Astrophysical Observatory
description: NAOJ facility known for the MuSCAT series of instruments that simultaneously observe exoplanet transits in multiple wavelengths.

name: Ondřejov Observatory
description: The primary observatory of the Czech Academy of Sciences. The Perek 2m telescope and stellar department are among the most productive in Central Europe.

name: Onsala Space Observatory
description: Chalmers University facility on the Swedish west coast. A major European VLBI station and host of the Onsala Twin Telescope for geodetic measurements.

name: Ooty Radio Telescope
description: A 530m-long cylindrical parabolic telescope on a hillside near Ooty. Built on an ingenious design to track celestial sources using Earth's rotation.

name: Oukaimeden Observatory
description: Cadi Ayyad University observatory in the Atlas Mountains. Home of TRAPPIST-North telescope, the northern counterpart to the system that discovered TRAPPIST-1 planets.

name: Owens Valley Radio Observatory
location: Owens Valley Radio Observatory, United States
description: Caltech's radio observatory in the Owens Valley. The 40m telescope's blazar monitoring program is the longest-running of its kind.

name: Palomar Observatory
description: Home of the historic Hale 200-inch telescope that dominated astronomy for decades. Now hosts the Zwicky Transient Facility for time-domain astronomy.

name: Pan-STARRS
description: Panoramic Survey Telescope and Rapid Response System on Haleakalā. Continuously scans the sky for moving and transient objects, discovering numerous asteroids and supernovae.

name: Paranal Observatory
description: ESO's flagship site at Cerro Paranal hosting the VLT, VISTA survey telescope, VST, and VLT Interferometer. One of the world's most productive observatory sites.

name: Observatoire de Paris
description: The oldest operating observatory in the world, founded in 1667. Now primarily a research institution coordinating French astronomical research.

name: Parkes Observatory (Murriyang)
description: Iconic 64m radio telescope in NSW, recently given the Wiradjuri name Murriyang. Discovered more pulsars than any other telescope and relayed Apollo 11 moonwalk images.

name: Pic du Midi Observatory
description: High-altitude French observatory in the Pyrenees with exceptional atmospheric stability. Pioneered coronagraphic observations and planetary imaging.

name: Pierre Auger Observatory
description: The world's largest cosmic ray observatory, covering 3000 km² in Mendoza province. Uses 1600 water-Cherenkov detectors and fluorescence telescopes.

name: Planck Science Office
description: The Planck satellite (2009-2013) produced the most detailed all-sky map of the cosmic microwave background, precisely measuring the age, composition, and geometry of the universe.

name: Pulkovo Observatory
description: Principal astronomical observatory of the Russian Academy of Sciences, founded in 1839. Known as the 'Astronomical Capital of the World' in the 19th century.

name: Purple Mountain Observatory
description: Historic Chinese observatory near Nanjing, founded in 1934. Known for asteroid and comet discoveries. Now focuses on near-Earth object surveys and solar physics.

name: Pushchino Radio Astronomy Observatory
description: Lebedev Physical Institute observatory south of Moscow. Operates several large radio telescope systems for low-frequency radio astronomy.

name: RATAN-600
description: A circular 576m-diameter radio telescope in the Caucasus. One of the largest radio telescopes in the world by aperture, operating since 1974.

name: Roque de los Muchachos Observatory
location: Roque de los Muchachos Observatory, Spain
description: One of the world's premier observatory sites on La Palma, hosting telescopes from multiple nations. Benefits from exceptional atmospheric conditions above the cloud layer.

name: Rozhen National Astronomical Observatory
description: Bulgaria's national observatory in the Rhodope Mountains. The 2m Ritchey-Chrétien telescope is the largest in Southeastern Europe.

name: Sutherland Observing Station
description: SAAO's primary observing station in the semi-arid Karoo. Hosts SALT and a suite of smaller telescopes used for time-domain astronomy and spectroscopy.

name: Southern African Large Telescope
location: Southern African Large Telescope, South Africa
description: The largest single optical telescope in the southern hemisphere, located in Sutherland. Its segmented primary mirror design is based on the Hobby-Eberly Telescope.

name: Observatorio Astronómico Nacional (San Pedro Mártir)
location: Observatorio Astronómico Nacional (San Pedro Mártir), Mexico
description: UNAM's primary observing site in Baja California. One of the darkest observatory sites in North America with excellent seeing conditions.

name: Sanglokh Observatory
description: Tajik National Observatory site at an altitude of 2300m. Specializes in astrometric observations and small body tracking.

name: Special Astrophysical Observatory
description: Home of the BTA-6, the largest telescope in Russia (6m aperture), located in the Caucasus Mountains. Also operates the RATAN-600 radio telescope.

name: Sardinia Radio Telescope
description: INAF's newest major radio telescope in Sardinia, capable of receiving frequencies from 300 MHz to 116 GHz. Also used for space debris monitoring.

name: Shamakhi Astrophysical Observatory
description: The main astronomical observatory of Azerbaijan, located in the Greater Caucasus mountains. Conducts stellar photometry and solar physics research.

name: Siding Spring Observatory
description: Australia's premier optical observatory site in the Warrumbungle Mountains, NSW. Hosts multiple telescopes including the Anglo-Australian Telescope.

name: Sierra Nevada Observatory
description: Instituto de Astrofísica de Andalucía observatory near Granada. High-altitude site specializing in photometry, infrared observations, and solar system studies.

name: Crimean Astrophysical Observatory
description: One of the largest observatories of the former Soviet Union. The Shajn 2.6m reflector has contributed to stellar and solar physics for decades.

name: SKA-Low
description: The low-frequency component of the Square Kilometre Array in Western Australia. Over 131,000 log-periodic dipole antennas will observe 50-350 MHz.

name: SKA-Mid
description: The mid-frequency component of the SKA in the Karoo. Will integrate 64 MeerKAT dishes with 133 new 15m dishes for unprecedented sensitivity.

name: Submillimeter Array
description: Joint venture of the Smithsonian and Academia Sinica on Mauna Kea. Eight 6m dishes provide high-resolution submillimeter observations.

name: SNOLAB
description: The world's deepest underground physics laboratory, 2 km below surface in Sudbury, Ontario. Expanded from the original SNO experiment that solved the solar neutrino problem.

name: South African Astronomical Observatory
location: South African Astronomical Observatory, South Africa
description: SAAO headquarters in Cape Town, coordinating South African optical astronomy. The observing station at Sutherland hosts SALT and other telescopes.

name: South Pole Telescope
description: A 10m microwave/millimeter telescope at the Amundsen-Scott South Pole Station. The dry, stable atmosphere makes it ideal for CMB observations.

name: Springbrook Research Observatory
description: University of Southern Queensland observatory in the Gold Coast hinterland. Used for research training and robotic monitoring observations.

name: ANU Siding Spring Station
description: Australian National University facility at Siding Spring. SkyMapper is creating the first comprehensive digital survey of the entire southern sky.

name: Space Telescope Science Institute
location: Space Telescope Science Institute, United States
description: Operations center for both HST and JWST at Johns Hopkins University. These two telescopes have produced many of the most iconic astronomical images and discoveries.

name: Subaru Telescope
description: National Astronomical Observatory of Japan's 8.2m telescope on Mauna Kea. Known for its wide-field imaging capability with the Hyper Suprime-Cam.

name: Suffa Radio Telescope
description: A 70m radio telescope under construction on the Suffa Plateau in Uzbekistan. Will be one of the world's largest radio telescopes when completed.

name: Sunspot Solar Observatory
description: Formerly Sacramento Peak Observatory, specializing in solar observation. The Dunn Solar Telescope is a premier instrument for studying solar surface phenomena.

name: Super-Kamiokande
description: A massive underground water Cherenkov detector in the Kamioka mine. Discovered that neutrinos have mass through observation of neutrino oscillations.

name: Penn State (Swift Observatory)
description: Mission operations for Swift at Penn State. Swift rapidly slews to gamma-ray bursts, providing crucial multi-wavelength observations of transient events.

name: Tartu Observatory
description: Estonian national observatory that pioneered studies of the cosmic web and galaxy superclusters under Jaan Einasto, revealing the large-scale structure of the universe.

name: Thüringer Landessternwarte
description: Thuringian State Observatory near Tautenburg, housing the Alfred Jensch telescope, the largest optical instrument in Germany.

name: Teide Observatory
description: Instituto de Astrofísica de Canarias facility on Tenerife. Specializes in solar physics, cosmic microwave background experiments, and infrared astronomy.

name: Telescope Array
description: Largest cosmic ray observatory in the Northern Hemisphere, in the Utah desert. Detected the Amaterasu particle, one of the highest-energy cosmic rays ever observed.

name: Thai National Observatory
description: NARIT's flagship facility on Doi Inthanon, Thailand's highest peak. The 2.4m telescope is the largest in Southeast Asia.

name: Tianma Radio Telescope
description: Shanghai Astronomical Observatory's 65m telescope at Sheshan. The largest fully steerable radio telescope in Asia.

name: Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex
location: Canberra Deep Space Communication Complex, Australia
description: NASA DSN station near Canberra. DSS-43 is the only antenna that can communicate with Voyager 2 and is used for radio astronomy observations.

name: Thirty Meter Telescope
description: An international observatory planned for Mauna Kea (with La Palma as alternative). Will be among the largest optical telescopes ever built.

name: Telescopio Nazionale Galileo
description: Italian national telescope on La Palma. HARPS-N, its high-precision spectrograph, has been crucial for measuring masses of transiting exoplanets discovered by Kepler and TESS.

name: Toruń Centre for Astronomy
description: Nicolaus Copernicus University astronomical center, one of the largest in Poland. Combines optical and radio astronomy research.

name: TRAPPIST-South
description: Belgian robotic telescope at La Silla that discovered the TRAPPIST-1 planetary system with 7 Earth-sized planets, including several in the habitable zone.

name: TUBITAK National Observatory
description: Turkey's national observatory at Bakırlıtepe, operating the Russian-Turkish RTT150 telescope for rapid transient follow-up observations.

name: Tuorla Observatory
description: University of Turku observatory known for long-term monitoring of blazars and active galactic nuclei, contributing to multi-wavelength campaigns.

name: Usuda Deep Space Center
description: JAXA's deep space communication facility with a 64m antenna. Supports Japanese space missions and participates in VLBI radio astronomy observations.

name: Vainu Bappu Observatory
description: Indian Institute of Astrophysics observatory in Kavalur, Tamil Nadu. The 2.3m telescope is the largest optical telescope in Asia's developing nations.

name: Vatican Advanced Technology Telescope
location: Vatican Advanced Technology Telescope, United States
description: The Vatican Observatory's research telescope on Mount Graham, Arizona. Operated by the Vatican Observatory Research Group with advanced CCD imaging capabilities.

name: Ventspils International Radio Astronomy Centre
location: Ventspils International Radio Astronomy Centre, Latvia
description: Converted Soviet space surveillance station in Latvia. The 32m and 16m antennas now serve as VLBI stations for the European VLBI Network.

name: Vera C. Rubin Observatory
keyDiscoveries: Expected: Legacy Survey of Space and Time
description: Formerly LSST. Will photograph the entire visible sky every few nights for 10 years, producing a 500 petabyte dataset to revolutionize time-domain astronomy.

name: VERITAS
description: Four 12m imaging atmospheric Cherenkov telescopes at Fred Lawrence Whipple Observatory. Key facility for very-high-energy gamma-ray astronomy.

name: Virgo Interferometer
description: European gravitational wave detector near Pisa. Adding Virgo to LIGO observations enables precise source localization, crucial for multi-messenger follow-up.

name: Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array
location: Karl G. Jansky Very Large Array, United States
description: 27 radio antennas in a Y-configuration on the Plains of San Agustin, New Mexico. One of the world's premier radio telescopes since 1980.

name: Very Large Telescope (VLT)
description: ESO's flagship facility at Cerro Paranal. Its four 8.2m Unit Telescopes can work independently or combine light interferometrically, making it the world's most productive ground-based observatory.

name: Westerbork Synthesis Radio Telescope
description: An array of 14 dish antennas in Drenthe. Pioneered radio aperture synthesis imaging and continues observations with the Apertif phased-array feed system.

name: William Herschel Telescope
description: The largest optical telescope in Europe when built in 1987. Part of the Isaac Newton Group on La Palma, specializing in spectroscopy and imaging.

name: Winer Observatory
description: Private research observatory in southeastern Arizona hosting robotic telescopes including KELT-North, which has discovered several transiting hot Jupiters.

name: Wise Observatory
description: Tel Aviv University observatory in the Negev Desert. Israel's largest optical telescope, active in exoplanet transit observations and asteroid studies.

name: Xinglong Station
description: NAOC's major observing station near Beijing. LAMOST, the Large Sky Area Multi-Object Fiber Spectroscopic Telescope, has obtained more stellar spectra than any other facility.

name: JAXA Sagamihara (XRISM)
description: JAXA's Institute of Space and Astronautical Science operates XRISM (X-Ray Imaging and Spectroscopy Mission), launched 2023, for high-resolution X-ray spectroscopy.

name: Yebes Observatory
description: IGN observatory in Guadalajara province. The 40m telescope specializes in molecular spectroscopy and VLBI. Part of the RAEGE geodetic network.

name: Yerkes Observatory
description: Houses the world's largest refracting telescope (40-inch). Founded in 1897 by George Ellery Hale, birthplace of modern astrophysics. Recently restored and reopened.

name: Yunnan Astronomical Observatory
description: CAS observatory in Kunming operating the 2.4m telescope at Lijiang and a 1m solar telescope. Focus on time-domain astronomy and solar activity.
